name: Cova de Sant Martí
starting_point: Located 100 m from the MA-3470 road between km 2 and 3. Access is signposted and you can park beside the entrance.
footwear: Appropriate footwear for entering a cave
equipment: No vertical progression equipment needed. Only helmet and lighting. Neoprene suit if entering the water.
description: Currently closed to the public. A natural cave linked to early Christian worship in Alcúdia, featuring two medieval Gothic chapels dedicated to Sant Martí and Sant Jordi built in the 13th-14th centuries after the Catalan conquest. Beyond the chapel area, the cave extends into passages with underground lakes inhabited by two protected bat species sensitive to noise and light.
full_description:
CURRENTLY CLOSED TO ACCESS

The Cova de Sant Martí is linked to the earliest Christian worship in this part of the island, according to historians.

The natural cave communicates with the outside through a staircase carved into the rock. In the large chamber, which is accessed by descending the staircase from the outside, there are two chapels formed by a semicircular arch with Gothic-style cross-ribbed vaulting from the medieval period, representing respectively Sant Martí and Sant Jordi, and they were built after the Catalan conquest during the 13th and 14th centuries.

In the main chamber, 15 meters below the exterior and next to the access stairs to the cave, there is a small metal barrier approximately eighty centimeters high on the taller side, oriented at approximately 320°, which gives access to the most interesting part of the cave where two species of protected bats live that are sensitive to noise and light.

Once past the barrier and inside the main chamber, which is approximately 15 meters long in the shape of a cross, at the center of which there is a mound that we will have to pass on one of the sides. The cavity is very humid and slippery, where immediately upon entering one can see a lake to the right that surrounds the entire right side of the cavern and which at the far end extends approximately 10 meters further. The cave extends deepest on the left side, where we will access immediately upon entering the cave and, reaching the previously mentioned mound, passing it on the left side with care not to fall into the water unless we are wearing a neoprene suit. Immediately, once on the other side, one can see that the cave actually continues after overcoming a small slope. The remaining stretch to explore is not at all comfortable, as we will have to advance sometimes crouching and other times even dragging our body along the ground. On our left, in the direction we are advancing, one can quickly see the water of the lake, which is the continuation of the one we skirted before, and which we can descend into through a small pit and follow to the end of the cavity.

The underwater part of the cave has been explored, as in one of the lakes there is a guide rope that goes into its depths, although I do not know the result of the exploration.

On the way back to the chamber where the two chapels are located, one can also access another lake of approximately 15 meters in length, which we will access by descending through a sort of crawlway, thus concluding the visit.
